questions.question: The First Great Awakening was a religious revival movement that swept through colonial America in the 1730s–1740s. What was its broader social impact beyond religion?
options: It led directly to the establishment of the first public school system in the colonies
It challenged traditional church authority and promoted a sense of personal spiritual equality that influenced later democratic thinking
It created the first inter-colonial institutions, which became blueprints for the Continental Congress
It suppressed dissenting religious views and consolidated Puritan dominance across all colonies
questions.answer: It challenged traditional church authority and promoted a sense of personal spiritual equality that influenced later democratic thinking
questions.explanation: Preachers like George Whitefield and Jonathan Edwards traveled across the colonies delivering emotionally charged sermons, drawing massive crowds and bypassing established church hierarchies. The movement emphasized personal conversion and a direct relationship with God, regardless of social standing — a spiritual egalitarianism that some historians see as a cultural precursor to the democratic ideals of the American Revolution.
questions.memory_hook: The Great Awakening (1730s–40s) bypassed church hierarchies and told colonists each person's faith mattered equally — a spiritual egalitarianism that fed the democratic spirit of 1776.

questions.question: The Anglo-Boer War (1899–1902) was fought in southern Africa between Britain and two Boer republics. It introduced a practice later condemned as a war crime. What was it?
options: Britain deployed poison gas against entrenched Boer positions for the first time in modern warfare
Britain invented the concentration camp, interning Boer civilians in camps where tens of thousands died
Britain pioneered aerial bombing using tethered observation balloons to drop explosives
Britain used scorched-earth tactics and deliberately starved civilian populations in besieged cities
questions.answer: Britain invented the concentration camp, interning Boer civilians in camps where tens of thousands died
questions.explanation: To deprive Boer guerrillas of support and supplies, British commander Lord Kitchener created a network of concentration camps for Boer women, children, and farm workers — as well as separate camps for Black Africans. Conditions were terrible: over 26,000 Boer civilians (mostly children) and an estimated 20,000 Black Africans died of disease and malnutrition. British journalist Emily Hobhouse exposed the camps, sparking international outrage.
questions.memory_hook: The Boer War (1899–1902) saw Britain invent the concentration camp to starve out guerrillas — 26,000+ Boer civilians, mostly children, died in them before Emily Hobhouse exposed the scandal.

questions.question: After the Civil War, three constitutional amendments fundamentally altered American society. What did the 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments collectively accomplish?
options: Abolished slavery, established birthright citizenship and equal protection, and granted Black men the right to vote
Abolished slavery, granted women the right to vote, and established federal income tax
Freed enslaved people, created the Supreme Court's power of judicial review, and banned poll taxes
Abolished indentured servitude, granted citizenship to Native Americans, and prohibited racial segregation
questions.answer: Abolished slavery, established birthright citizenship and equal protection, and granted Black men the right to vote
questions.explanation: The 13th Amendment (1865) abolished slavery. The 14th Amendment (1868) defined citizenship (overturning Dred Scott), established equal protection under the law, and due process. The 15th Amendment (1870) prohibited denying the vote based on race. Together they are the Reconstruction Amendments — though their promises were systematically undermined by Jim Crow laws until the Civil Rights Movement nearly a century later.
questions.memory_hook: The three Reconstruction Amendments (13th, 14th, 15th) abolished slavery, created equal citizenship, and gave Black men the vote — promises that Jim Crow spent 90 years trying to erase.

questions.question: The Paralympic Games have grown into the world's second-largest multi-sport event. Where and when were the first Games held, and what inspired them?
options: London 1948, inspired by the rehabilitation needs of World War II veterans with disabilities
Rome 1960, growing out of the Stoke Mandeville Games for spinal injury patients organized by Dr. Ludwig Guttmann
Tokyo 1964, created by the Japanese government to show disability inclusion alongside the Olympic Games
Helsinki 1952, inspired by Finnish soldiers disabled during the Winter War against the Soviet Union
questions.answer: Rome 1960, growing out of the Stoke Mandeville Games for spinal injury patients organized by Dr. Ludwig Guttmann
questions.explanation: Dr. Ludwig Guttmann, a Jewish neurosurgeon who fled Nazi Germany for Britain, organized archery competitions for WWII veterans with spinal injuries at Stoke Mandeville Hospital in 1948. These evolved into international competitions and the first Paralympic Games were held in Rome in 1960 alongside the Olympics, with 400 athletes from 23 countries. The name 'Paralympic' derives from the Greek prefix 'para' (beside), meaning the Games run parallel to the Olympics.
questions.memory_hook: The Paralympics grew from Dr. Guttmann's 1948 archery games for paralyzed WWII veterans at Stoke Mandeville — by Rome 1960 they were officially alongside the Olympics as a second global games.

questions.question: In 2005, Danica Patrick made history at the Indianapolis 500. What did she achieve?
options: Became the first woman to win the Indianapolis 500
Became the first woman to lead laps at the Indianapolis 500 and finished fourth — the highest finish by a woman in race history at that time
Became the first woman to qualify for the Indianapolis 500 pole position
Set the fastest qualifying speed in Indianapolis 500 history
questions.answer: Became the first woman to lead laps at the Indianapolis 500 and finished fourth — the highest finish by a woman in race history at that time
questions.explanation: Patrick led 19 laps at the 2005 Indy 500 — the first woman to lead laps at the race — and finished fourth, the best result ever by a female driver at Indianapolis at the time. She came close to winning (she was running first with 10 laps to go) but had to make a fuel stop. Patrick later became the first woman to win an IndyCar Series race (2008 Motegi) and the first to win an NASCAR Cup Series pole.
questions.memory_hook: Danica Patrick led laps at the 2005 Indy 500 — the first woman to do so — and nearly won before a fuel stop dropped her to fourth, still the best female Indy finish at the time.

questions.question: Vittorio De Sica's 1948 Italian film Bicycle Thieves (Ladri di biciclette) is a masterpiece of neorealist cinema. What is 'neorealism' and why did it emerge in Italy?
options: A style using only natural light and non-professional actors, emerging in postwar Italy to depict ordinary people's struggles with honesty
A theoretical film movement rejecting all narrative convention, emerging from Italian universities after Mussolini's fall
A style using elaborate studio recreations of real locations, developed to preserve Italy's film industry after WWII damage
A movement requiring directors to document real events without any scripting, influenced by American documentary filmmakers
questions.answer: A style using natural light and non-professional actors, emerging in postwar Italy to depict ordinary people's struggles with honesty
questions.explanation: Italian neorealism emerged after WWII's devastation, when filmmakers shot on location in bombed-out streets using non-professional actors to tell stories about poverty and survival. Bicycle Thieves follows a poor man and his young son searching Rome for a stolen bicycle essential to his job. De Sica's restrained, observational style influenced filmmakers worldwide — from the French New Wave to modern Iranian cinema.
questions.memory_hook: Italian neorealism used real streets, non-professional actors, and natural light to show postwar poverty honestly — Bicycle Thieves' father-son bike search across Rome influenced every realist filmmaker who followed.

questions.question: Billy Wilder's 1950 film Sunset Boulevard presents a dark portrait of Hollywood and faded stardom. What is the film's famous opening device?
options: The film begins with a documentary crew interviewing studio executives about Hollywood's golden age
The film is narrated by the main character Joe Gillis — who is already dead, floating face-down in a swimming pool
The film opens with a silent film within the film, transitioning to sound to mark Hollywood's own transition
The film begins with an auction of a dead actress's belongings, which are sold one by one to reveal her story
questions.answer: The film is narrated by the main character Joe Gillis — who is already dead, floating face-down in a swimming pool
questions.explanation: Sunset Boulevard opens with police arriving at a mansion to find a body floating in a pool. The dead man — failed screenwriter Joe Gillis (William Holden) — then narrates his own story in flashback, explaining how he ended up there. Gloria Swanson plays Norma Desmond, a delusional silent film star who has never accepted her career's end. The film remains one of cinema's most scathing critiques of the entertainment industry.
questions.memory_hook: Sunset Boulevard (1950) has one of cinema's great narrative twists hidden in its opening — the narrator is already dead, floating in a pool, telling us how he got there.

questions.question: François Truffaut's 1959 film The 400 Blows is considered the landmark film of the French New Wave. What does the title mean, and what is the film about?
options: It refers to the number of films released in France in 1958, and follows a film critic discovering them
The title is a French idiom meaning 'to raise hell'; the film follows a troubled 13-year-old delinquent in Paris partly based on Truffaut's own childhood
It refers to 400 blows of a hammer used to build a ship in a story about French maritime history
The title is symbolic; the film follows 400 diverse Parisians across a single day in the city
questions.answer: The title is a French idiom meaning 'to raise hell'; the film follows a troubled 13-year-old delinquent in Paris partly based on Truffaut's own childhood
questions.explanation: Faire les quatre cents coups is a French expression meaning to engage in wild or delinquent behavior. The film follows Antoine Doinel, a neglected 13-year-old who skips school, steals, and eventually ends up in a juvenile detention center — all drawn from Truffaut's own difficult childhood. It launched the French New Wave and introduced a new cinematic language of handheld cameras, location shooting, and naturalistic acting.
questions.memory_hook: The 400 Blows (1959) means 'raising hell' in French — Truffaut's autobiographical portrait of a neglected Parisian teenager launched the French New Wave and changed cinema's visual language.

questions.question: The Niger Delta in West Africa is one of the world's largest river deltas. What combination of factors makes it globally significant?
options: It is the world's most biodiverse marine ecosystem, with more species than the Amazon estuary
It contains massive oil reserves and is one of Africa's most densely populated river deltas, but oil extraction has caused severe environmental destruction
It is the world's largest producer of freshwater fish, feeding over 500 million people across West Africa
It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site recognized for preserving the largest intact mangrove forests in Africa
questions.answer: It contains massive oil reserves and is one of Africa's most densely populated river deltas, but oil extraction has caused severe environmental destruction
questions.explanation: The Niger Delta holds some of Africa's largest oil reserves — Nigeria is one of the world's top oil exporters. However, decades of oil spills, gas flaring, and pipeline leaks have devastated the delta's ecosystems and the livelihoods of millions of fishing communities. The delta's Ogoni people became internationally known through the activist Ken Saro-Wiwa, who was executed by the Nigerian military government in 1995 for protesting Shell's environmental damage.
questions.memory_hook: The Niger Delta sits atop Nigeria's oil wealth but decades of spills have poisoned what fishing communities depended on — Ken Saro-Wiwa was executed in 1995 for protesting Shell's destruction of Ogoni lands.

questions.question: Lake Malawi (also called Lake Nyasa) in southeastern Africa is remarkable for its biodiversity. What makes it scientifically exceptional?
options: It is the world's oldest lake, providing a continuous fossil record of aquatic evolution spanning 20 million years
It contains more freshwater fish species than any other lake on Earth, mostly cichlids found nowhere else
It is the world's deepest lake outside Asia, with zones of water never exposed to oxygen
It is the only lake where the water level rises in winter and falls in summer, reversed from all other African lakes
questions.answer: It contains more freshwater fish species than any other lake on Earth, mostly cichlids found nowhere else
questions.explanation: Lake Malawi holds approximately 700–1,000 species of fish, the vast majority of which are cichlids found nowhere else in the world. This extraordinary diversity evolved in relative isolation over millions of years and is a textbook example of adaptive radiation — the rapid diversification of a single ancestor species into many forms filling different ecological niches. The lake borders Malawi, Tanzania, and Mozambique.
questions.memory_hook: Lake Malawi holds 700–1,000 fish species — more than any other lake — mostly endemic cichlids that evolved in isolation, a living textbook example of adaptive radiation.

questions.question: Ecological succession describes how ecosystems change over time. What is the difference between primary and secondary succession?
options: Primary succession begins in areas of existing soil; secondary succession begins on bare rock with no soil
Primary succession begins on bare substrate with no soil (like fresh lava or glacial retreat); secondary succession occurs where an ecosystem was disrupted but soil remains
Primary succession involves only plants; secondary succession involves plants and animals colonizing together
Primary succession happens in aquatic environments; secondary succession happens on land
questions.answer: Primary succession begins on bare substrate with no soil (like fresh lava or glacial retreat); secondary succession occurs where an ecosystem was disrupted but soil remains
questions.explanation: Primary succession begins where no soil exists — such as volcanic rock after a lava flow, or land exposed after a glacier retreats. Pioneer species like lichens and mosses break down rock into soil over decades. Secondary succession occurs in disturbed areas where soil already exists, such as after a forest fire or abandoned farmland — allowing faster recovery because soil microbes and seed banks remain. Both progress toward a stable climax community.
questions.memory_hook: Primary succession starts on bare rock (no soil) — lichens lead the way; secondary succession starts on disrupted soil (after fire or farming), so it recovers much faster.

questions.question: Marie Curie's discovery of radioactivity earned her two Nobel Prizes. What are the two types of radioactive decay she helped identify, and what is emitted in each?
options: Alpha decay (emits helium nuclei) and beta decay (emits electrons or positrons)
Gamma decay (emits high-energy photons) and delta decay (emits neutrons)
Proton decay (emits protons) and neutron decay (emits neutrons)
Alpha decay (emits neutrons) and gamma decay (emits helium nuclei)
questions.answer: Alpha decay (emits helium nuclei) and beta decay (emits electrons or positrons)
questions.explanation: Curie, working with her husband Pierre, identified and named two types of radioactive decay: alpha decay, in which a nucleus emits an alpha particle (2 protons + 2 neutrons = helium nucleus), and beta decay, in which a nucleus emits an electron (beta-minus) or positron (beta-plus). A third type, gamma decay (emission of high-energy photons), was also characterized in this era. Curie won the Nobel Prize in Physics (1903) and Chemistry (1911) — the first person to win two Nobel Prizes.
questions.memory_hook: Curie identified alpha decay (helium nuclei) and beta decay (electrons) — she won two Nobel Prizes (Physics 1903, Chemistry 1911) and remains the only person to win in two different sciences.
